title: Accountants and auditors
rationale: An AI agent for accountants/auditors requires deep integration with each client's general ledger, ERP (NetSuite, SAP, QuickBooks), tax filing systems, and document management, plus configuration to the company's chart of accounts, policies, and regulatory regime. The skills are standardized (GAAP, tax code) but useful deployment demands per-customer data plumbing, controls, and audit-trail compliance — matching the Harvey/Abridge pattern. Forensic and advisory work has tacit elements, but the bulk of the occupation fits T2.

title: Actors
rationale: Acting is a performative occupation where value lies in the embodied performance itself, not knowledge work outputs.

title: Actuaries
rationale: Actuarial work requires deep integration with company-specific data (policy databases, claims history, mortality tables, pension records) and tuning to each insurer's product lines, regulatory jurisdiction, and reserving methodologies. While the mathematical and statistical skills are highly standardized, an AI agent serving an actuarial function would need substantial per-customer configuration to access internal data warehouses, replicate company-specific models, and integrate with regulatory reporting workflows. The agent product is genericizable across the industry but each enterprise deployment requires significant integration work.

title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
rationale: Teaching adult basic education and ESL involves standard curricula (high school equivalency subjects, English language fundamentals) and uses common tools (Canvas, standard lesson materials). A horizontal AI tutor agent could deliver lessons, assess progress, and adapt instruction across any program without per-customer integration. The in-person classroom and relational components are real but the core knowledge work — lesson planning, content delivery, assessment — is highly genericizable.

title: Advertising sales agents
rationale: Advertising sales is fundamentally relational: success depends on cultivating trust with specific clients, repeated cold-call persistence, and rapport-based negotiation. An AI agent could automate prospect research, proposal drafting, media kit prep, and pipeline tracking, but the core sales relationship and persuasion calls remain with the human. This makes it a T3c relational role rather than a genericizable or framework-customizable one.

title: Advertising, promotions, and marketing managers
rationale: Marketing managers' work combines strategic decision-making with relational coordination across internal teams (sales, finance, executives), external clients, agencies, and media partners. While agents can augment portable tasks like market research, campaign analysis, copy drafting, and media planning, the core managerial work involves negotiating contracts, persuading stakeholders, and maintaining client/agency relationships that stay with the human. There's also a tacit judgment component (brand instinct, creative direction) but the relational dimension dominates at this management level.

title: Aerospace engineers
rationale: Aerospace engineering involves deep tacit judgment about safety-critical systems (structural margins, aerodynamic intuition, failure modes) developed over years on specific airframes and programs, combined with proprietary CAD/CAE toolchains, classified defense data, and program-specific design heritage that can't be cleanly extracted. An agent useful here would need to be harness-engineered by the senior engineers themselves, iterating on their own simulation and design workflows. While some sub-tasks (documentation, basic analysis) could be genericized, the core design judgment resists articulation and standardization.

title: Air traffic controllers
rationale: Air traffic control is knowledge work (decision-making, communication, monitoring), but an agent would require deep integration with FAA-specific radar systems, NextGen infrastructure, airspace configurations, and facility-specific procedures. The skills are standardized across the industry but deployment requires substantial per-facility configuration and certification against safety-critical systems. Note: actual deployment is also constrained by regulation, but the shape is T2.

title: Announcers and DJs
rationale: Announcers and DJs are primarily performative — their value lies in voice, personality, live presence, and entertaining audiences in real time. While there is some research and writing involved, the core output is performance for an audience, not knowledge artifacts.

title: Arbitrators, mediators, and conciliators
rationale: The core work is facilitating dialogue between disputing parties, requiring trust, neutrality, and in-person relational presence with emotionally charged humans. While an agent could assist with document review, precedent research, and drafting settlement agreements, the relational core—building rapport, reading the room, and being accepted as a neutral third party—must remain with the human. Parties consent to a specific mediator/arbitrator, making this fundamentally relational.

title: Architects
rationale: Architecture combines client relationships, creative judgment, and site-specific tacit knowledge with standard tools (CADD/BIM). While drafting and code-checking can be done by horizontal agents on standard files, the core work involves sustained client relationships to elicit requirements, negotiate design tradeoffs, and manage contractor relationships through construction — value that lives in specific relational trust. The portable parts (drawings, specs, code research) are augmentable T1-style, but the role's core is relational.

title: Architectural and engineering managers
rationale: A&E managers combine technical judgment (evaluating designs, anticipating failure modes) with relational leadership (hiring, motivating teams, stakeholder coordination) and deep company/project-specific tacit context. Much of the value comes from engineering gestalt that even the manager can't fully articulate, so only the expert themselves could harness an agent to replicate it. Relational and T3a elements are present, but the genuinely tacit technical judgment dominates.

title: Art directors
rationale: Art directors' work centers on translating client vision into creative direction and leading teams of artists — value lives in client relationships, taste negotiation, and stakeholder management. An AI agent could augment portable parts (mood boards, layout drafts, reviewing artwork) but the relational core of pitching to clients and directing creative staff stays with the human. Aspects are also tacit (creative judgment), but the relational dimension dominates.

title: Atmospheric scientists, including meteorologists
rationale: Atmospheric scientists work with standard meteorological data sources (NOAA models, radar, satellites) but operational deployment requires integration with specific data feeds, forecasting systems, and client-specific needs (airline routing, utility load forecasting, broadcast graphics pipelines). An AI agent could be built as a product but each deployment requires connecting to the customer's data infrastructure and tuning outputs to their decision workflows. Research meteorology tasks lean more horizontal, but the operational forecasting majority fits T2.

title: Bill and account collectors
rationale: An AI agent for debt collection requires deep integration with each client's account systems, CRM, dialer/telephony infrastructure, and compliance configuration for FDCPA and state-specific rules. The core skills (negotiation, scripted communication) are generic, but per-deployment work — connecting to creditor databases, tuning escalation logic, configuring legal compliance guardrails per jurisdiction — is substantial. This matches T2: same agent product, heavy per-customer configuration.

title: Biochemists and biophysicists
rationale: Much of the work involves wet-lab experimentation and tacit scientific judgment — designing experiments, interpreting ambiguous results, and developing research intuition that even expert scientists struggle to articulate. While literature review, data analysis, and writing are genericizable, the core research craft requires the expert themselves to harness-engineer and iterate with an agent. Physical lab work also bottlenecks full automation.

title: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers
rationale: Bioengineering work involves designing physical medical devices, implants, and biological systems where deep tacit expertise (clinical intuition, material behavior, biological response) intersects with hands-on lab/clinical iteration. Much of the value comes from expert judgment that's hard to fully articulate—the engineer's feel for whether a device will perform safely in vivo—and requires close human-in-the-loop harness engineering. While portions of the work (simulation, documentation, literature review) are genericizable, the core design and validation work requires expert-led agent iteration.

title: Bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks
rationale: An AI agent for bookkeeping needs deep integration into each company's accounting software (QuickBooks, Xero, NetSuite), bank feeds, payroll systems, and chart of accounts, plus configuration to the client's specific categorization rules and reconciliation workflows. The skills are standardized but per-customer setup (connecting ledgers, mapping accounts, tuning approval/escalation logic) is substantial. Products like Pilot and Digits exemplify this T2 shape.

title: Budget analysts
rationale: Budget analysts work with organization-specific financial systems (ERP, government accounting systems), program structures, and approval workflows that require integration and configuration per customer. While the analytical skills are transferable, an agent must be wired into the company's chart of accounts, historical budgets, and reporting formats, plus tuned to its compliance rules and stakeholder communication norms.

title: Cartographers and photogrammetrists
rationale: Cartography agents would work with GIS/CAD platforms (ArcGIS, QGIS) on customer-specific spatial datasets, projection conventions, and map style guides. While the software is industry-standard, each deployment (local government, engineering firm) requires integration with the customer's spatial databases, data sources (LiDAR feeds, survey systems), and cartographic standards — closer to a configured vertical agent than a horizontal tool.

title: Cashiers
rationale: Cashier work is primarily physical and transactional—scanning items, handling cash, bagging purchases, and standing at a checkout station. Automation here comes through self-checkout hardware and payment systems, not knowledge-work AI agents.

title: Chemists and materials scientists
rationale: While computational and analytical portions could be genericized, much of the work depends on physical lab experimentation and tacit experimental intuition (which reactions to try, interpreting anomalous results, hands-on technique) that even experts struggle to articulate. An effective agent would need to be harness-engineered by the scientist themselves, integrated with proprietary lab equipment and iterated closely on bench results. The physical-experimental and tacit-judgment core prevents a clean T2 productization.

title: Claims adjusters, appraisers, examiners, and investigators
rationale: Claims work requires deep integration with each insurer's policy systems, claims management platforms, fraud detection databases, and company-specific underwriting rules. An agent (like those being built by insurtech vendors) uses similar skills across carriers but must be configured against each customer's policies, escalation workflows, and regulatory compliance setup. Field inspection tasks add some physical component, but the core evaluative/documentary work fits T2.

title: Compensation and benefits managers
rationale: An AI agent for this role needs deep integration with the company's HRIS, payroll, benefits vendors, and compensation databases, plus tuning to the organization's specific pay philosophy, job architecture, and regulatory context. The skills are generalizable across companies, but each deployment requires substantial per-customer configuration of data integrations, policy rules, and compliance controls — the classic T2 shape.

title: Compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists
rationale: An AI agent for this work would need deep integration with the employer's HRIS (Workday, ADP), compensation survey data subscriptions (Mercer, Radford), benefits carrier systems, and the company's specific job architecture and pay bands. The core skills (analyzing pay data, classifying jobs, ensuring compliance) are transferable across companies, but useful deployment requires per-customer configuration of pay structures, policies, and regulatory contexts. Same product, custom configuration per employer.

title: Compliance officers
rationale: Compliance work requires deep integration with company-specific policies, regulatory frameworks for the industry, internal document repositories, audit/training systems, and escalation workflows. While the agent product (regulatory tracking, audit, policy drafting, training generation) is similar across firms, each deployment requires substantial configuration to the customer's regulatory regime, internal controls, and reporting systems. The skills are generic but the agent must be wired into each organization's compliance stack.

title: Computer and information research scientists
rationale: Research scientists produce novel ideas, theories, and experimental designs at the frontier of computing — the tacit judgment about what problems matter, which approaches will work, and how to interpret ambiguous results is largely inarticulable and lives with the individual researcher. An agent here is best built by the researcher themselves through harness engineering and tight iteration, augmenting literature review, coding experiments, and analysis. Horizontal agents help with subtasks, but the creative research core resists genericization or extraction.

title: Computer and information systems managers
rationale: IT manager work centers on relational and organizational judgment: aligning IT strategy with executives, directing staff, negotiating with vendors, and making resource allocation decisions specific to the organization's politics and priorities. An agent could augment portable parts (vendor analysis, project planning, status reports) but the core leadership, stakeholder relationships, and accountability stay with the human manager.

title: Computer hardware engineers
rationale: Hardware engineering involves deep tacit expertise about physical design trade-offs, signal integrity, timing closure, thermal behavior, and manufacturability that experienced engineers often can't fully articulate. While EDA tools are somewhat standardized, the work is tightly coupled to proprietary chip architectures, fab process nodes, and accumulated design intuition that resists extraction. An effective agent would need to be harness-engineered by the expert engineers themselves, iterating on specific design problems rather than deployed as a generic or configured product.

title: Computer network architects
rationale: Network architects design networks tightly coupled to a specific organization's existing infrastructure, vendor stack, security posture, and physical/cloud topology. An agent for this work would need integrations into the company's network management systems, configuration databases, and vendor APIs, plus tuning to organizational standards — same product across customers but heavy per-deployment configuration. Skills are transferable but the agent cannot do useful work without substantial customer-specific context wiring.

title: Computer programmers
rationale: Programming work uses standard tools (IDEs, git, compilers) and codebases accessible via the file system. A horizontal coding agent like Claude Code or Cursor can do the work at any company by simply being pointed at the codebase—no per-customer integration or system-prompt tuning required.

title: Computer support specialists
rationale: An AI agent for computer support (think Decagon/Intercom Fin for IT helpdesk) requires deep per-customer integration: ticketing systems (ServiceNow, Jira), the company's specific software stack, network monitoring tools, knowledge bases of internal procedures, and escalation logic tuned to the org. The skills are generic but each deployment needs months of configuration to access the right systems and learn company-specific issues.

title: Computer systems analysts
rationale: Computer systems analysts require deep knowledge of an organization's specific existing systems, business processes, stakeholders, and industry context to design improvements — this tacit organizational knowledge dominates the work and prevents a horizontal agent from being useful. However, this context could be extracted through structured discovery (interviewing stakeholders, analyzing system documentation, mapping data flows), after which the agent would resemble a T2 product with per-company integrations into the org's IT stack. The liaison/translation work between management and IT is documentable rather than genuinely ineffable.

title: Cost estimators
rationale: Cost estimation requires standard skills (reading blueprints, analyzing materials/labor costs) but an AI agent must integrate with company-specific estimation software, historical cost databases, supplier pricing systems, and BIM/CAD tooling. Each company has proprietary cost benchmarks and bidding workflows that require configuration, making this a genericizable framework with custom per-customer deployment.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
rationale: Court reporting and captioning is fundamentally speech-to-text transcription with standardized outputs (verbatim transcripts, closed captions) across all customers. An AI agent (ASR + diarization + legal/domain vocabulary) works the same way at any court or broadcast — no per-customer integration is needed beyond access to the audio feed. The work is highly genericizable, which is why ASR technology is already displacing the role.

title: Credit counselors
rationale: Credit counselors do largely standardized advisory work (assessing finances, building debt management plans, explaining options), but an agent serving this role at scale needs integration with each employer's client management system, creditor negotiation workflows, and compliance/regulatory configurations (especially in housing or student aid contexts). The skills are transferable but the deployment requires per-customer system integration and tuning, fitting the T2 pattern.

title: Customer service representatives
rationale: A CSR agent (e.g., Decagon, Sierra, Ada) is a generic framework but requires per-customer integration into the company's CRM/ticketing systems (Zendesk, Salesforce), knowledge bases, and escalation workflows, plus system-prompt tuning on company-specific products, policies, and tone. The skills are transferable but each enterprise deployment involves substantial configuration and eval work, which is the defining T2 shape.

title: Data scientists
rationale: A data science agent needs access to the company's specific data warehouses, schemas, business metrics definitions, and domain context to produce useful analysis — generic horizontal agents can't meaningfully analyze a company's data without integration into their data stack (Snowflake, dbt, BI tools) and configuration around their KPIs. The skills are transferable but each deployment requires substantial per-customer setup of data connections, semantic layers, and stakeholder context. This mirrors the T2 pattern where the agent product is similar across companies but enterprise deployment requires meaningful integration work.

title: Database administrators and architects
rationale: DBA work requires deep integration with a company's specific database systems, schemas, security policies, and operational tooling. While the skills (SQL, performance tuning, backup/restore) are transferable, an AI agent serving a customer needs configured access to their particular DBMS instances, internal data models, and compliance controls — making it a genericizable framework with substantial per-customer configuration.

title: Desktop publishers
rationale: Desktop publishing uses standard software (InDesign, QuarkXPress, etc.) with standard inputs (text, graphics) and outputs (print-ready or web files) across all employers. A horizontal AI agent with access to these standard tools and the project files could handle layout work at any company without per-customer integration or configuration. The work is highly genericizable, similar to graphic design or copywriting.

title: Drafters
rationale: Drafters convert engineer/architect sketches into technical CAD/BIM drawings using standard industry tools (AutoCAD, Revit, SolidWorks). An AI agent connected to these standard CAD file formats can perform the work across firms without per-customer integration — the project files themselves provide the context. Specialization by domain (mechanical/architectural/electrical) is captured in the agent's training, not in customer-specific configuration.

title: Economists
rationale: Economist work — research, statistical analysis, modeling, forecasting, and writing reports — uses standard tools (Stata, R, Python, Excel) and produces standard outputs (papers, forecasts, policy memos). A horizontal agent with access to the economist's data files and standard analysis tools can do the work without per-employer integration. While some government roles have specific data systems, the core analytical work is genericizable across employers.

title: Editors
rationale: Editing work uses standard tools (Word, Google Docs, CMS platforms) and standard inputs/outputs (manuscripts in, edited text out) across publishers. A horizontal AI agent given access to the manuscript files can perform copyediting, fact-checking, and revision suggestions without per-customer integration. Style-guide adherence is just a prompt/reference document, not a deep enterprise integration.

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
rationale: Electrical/electronics engineering involves deep tacit judgment about physical systems—knowing whether a circuit will have signal integrity issues, EMI problems, thermal failures, or manufacturability concerns. While CAD tools are standard, the engineering intuition for tradeoffs in real hardware often can't be fully articulated and requires close iteration with the expert. Unlike software where the artifact is fully digital and testable, hardware involves physical site visits, supervising manufacturing, and embodied judgment that resists full extraction.

title: Epidemiologists
rationale: An epidemiologist agent needs to integrate with specific public health data systems (state/local surveillance databases, EHRs, CDC reporting pipelines) and be configured to a jurisdiction's reporting requirements, disease priorities, and stakeholder communication channels. The analytical skills (biostatistics, study design, report writing) are generic across employers, but useful deployment requires per-customer data integrations and workflow tuning. This matches T2 rather than T1 (not pure horizontal tool use) or T3 (knowledge is largely codified in epidemiological methods).

title: Fashion designers
rationale: Fashion design uses standard creative tools (CAD programs like CLO 3D, Adobe Illustrator, graphics editing software) that are common across the industry. An AI agent could assist with trend research, sketching, fabric selection, and design iteration by connecting to these standard tools without per-enterprise integration. The work output (designs, prototypes, tech packs) is portable across employers.

title: Film and video editors and camera operators
rationale: Camera operators are primarily physical work (operating cameras on location, often carrying heavy equipment). Film/video editing is knowledge work but the role as a whole is mixed and predominantly creative/physical craft rather than knowledge work amenable to agent deployment.

title: Financial analysts
rationale: An AI agent for financial analysts needs to ingest enterprise-specific data (internal financial models, proprietary research databases, Bloomberg/FactSet/Capital IQ feeds, portfolio management systems) and be tuned to the firm's investment thesis, risk frameworks, and reporting templates. While the analytical skills are generalizable, deployment requires substantial per-firm integration with data providers and internal systems, plus configuration to firm-specific methodologies. This is the classic T2 shape: same agent product, months of integration per enterprise.

title: Financial clerks
rationale: Financial clerks do transactional record-keeping work tied to enterprise-specific systems (billing platforms, payroll systems, EHRs, insurance policy databases, brokerage back-office systems). An AI agent for this work requires per-enterprise integrations into those systems plus configuration of the company's specific workflows, fee schedules, and approval rules. The skills are generic but deployment demands substantial customization per customer.

title: Financial examiners
rationale: Financial examiners review balance sheets, loan documents, and compliance with regulations at specific institutions. An AI agent would need integration into each institution's core banking systems, loan management platforms, and document repositories, plus tuning to the specific regulatory frameworks (federal vs state, risk vs consumer compliance) the examiner operates under. The skills generalize across employers but deployment requires substantial per-institution integration and configuration.

title: Financial managers
rationale: Financial managers analyze data, prepare reports, and advise executives — work that requires deep integration with enterprise-specific ERP/accounting systems (SAP, Oracle, NetSuite), internal financial databases, and industry/regulatory context (healthcare billing, government appropriations). An AI agent would need per-enterprise integrations and configuration to ledgers, chart of accounts, and reporting workflows, plus tuning to the org's policies and compliance rules. There is also a relational/advisory component with executives, but the core analytical work is a genericizable framework with heavy custom configuration.

title: Fundraisers
rationale: Fundraising centers on cultivating and maintaining personal relationships with donors—especially major gifts and planned giving—where trust, rapport, and in-person engagement are the core value. An AI agent can handle research, prospect identification, drafting appeals, and campaign analytics, but the relational core of soliciting and stewarding donors stays with the human.

title: General office clerks
rationale: An agent for general office clerks would handle phone routing, scheduling, document filing/retrieval, data entry, and bill processing — tasks that look similar across companies but depend on each employer's specific systems (phone systems, CRM, document repositories, EHRs for hospitals, SIS for colleges). A horizontal agent can't just plug in; deployment requires integrating with the enterprise's office stack and tuning workflows to local procedures. The skills are generic but the per-enterprise integration burden makes it T2.

title: Geographers
rationale: Geographers' work centers on GIS analysis, remote sensing, and spatial data interpretation using standard tools (ArcGIS, QGIS), but deployment at a government agency or firm requires integration with that organization's proprietary geospatial databases, satellite/imagery feeds, and domain-specific data layers (population, infrastructure, environmental). An agent product would generalize across geographers but need per-enterprise data pipeline configuration and workflow tuning. This puts it in T2 rather than T1.

title: Graphic designers
rationale: Graphic design uses standard professional tools (Adobe Creative Suite, Figma) that are used across every company. A horizontal AI agent given access to brand assets and project files could produce designs at any enterprise without per-customer integration work. The inputs (briefs, references) and outputs (visual files) are standardized across the industry.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
rationale: The work requires deep integration with enterprise-specific EHR systems (Epic, Cerner), clinical databases, and disease registries, plus tuning to institution-specific coding practices, data schemas, and compliance requirements (HIPAA). An agent product could serve many healthcare organizations but each deployment requires substantial integration into the hospital's specific information systems and validation workflows, mirroring the T2 pattern of medical scribing tools like Abridge.

title: High school teachers
rationale: Teaching is fundamentally relational — value comes from sustained relationships with specific students, classroom management, motivation, and in-person supervision. An AI agent can augment portable parts (lesson planning, grading, generating materials) but the core instructional and relational work stays with the human teacher in a physical classroom.

title: Historians
rationale: Historian work involves research, analysis, and writing using standard sources (archives, documents, books) and standard authoring tools. A horizontal AI agent with research, document analysis, and writing capabilities can perform this work across employers by being pointed at the relevant archives/sources, without per-enterprise integration or system-prompt tuning.

title: Human resources managers
rationale: HR managers' work centers on relationships with executives, employees, and union counterparts—mediating disputes, advising leaders, handling grievances, and serving as the trusted link between management and staff. While an agent could automate portable pieces (policy drafting, recruiting workflows, payroll oversight, compliance checks), the core consultative and mediation work depends on established trust and standing within the organization. Deployment would also require enterprise-specific integrations (HRIS, payroll), but the binding constraint is relational.

title: Human resources specialists
rationale: An HR agent needs deep integration with enterprise-specific systems (ATS like Workday/Greenhouse, HRIS, payroll, benefits providers) and tuning to each organization's policies, compliance requirements, and workflows. The core skills (screening, interviewing, policy guidance) are similar across employers, but deployment requires per-enterprise configuration of integrations, policy knowledge bases, and escalation logic. This matches the T2 pattern of a generic agent product with substantial customization per customer.

title: Industrial designers
rationale: Industrial designers work primarily in standard professional tools (CAD/CAID software like SolidWorks, Rhino, Figma for UI) that an AI agent can interface with the same way at any firm. The work product (sketches, 3D models, prototypes, specs) is portable across employers and doesn't require per-enterprise system integration. While some tacit aesthetic judgment exists, the core design workflow is genericizable like other creative/engineering tool-based roles.

title: Industrial engineers
rationale: Industrial engineering work depends heavily on tacit knowledge of specific factory layouts, production processes, worker behaviors, and physical observation of operations — none of which a horizontal agent can access. However, this context is documentable through process maps, time studies, sensor data, and structured interviews, which once captured could enable a per-enterprise agent (T2-like) to analyze data and recommend optimizations. The physical observation and cross-functional collaboration aspects make it harder than pure software roles, placing it in T3a.

title: Information clerks
rationale: Information clerks do routine clerical work that's similar across employers in skill terms, but automating it requires deep integration with each enterprise's specific systems — hotel PMS, court docketing systems, government eligibility databases, HRIS, order-entry platforms. A clerk-automation agent product can serve many enterprises, but each deployment requires connecting to bespoke records systems and tuning workflows to local policies and escalation rules.

title: Information security analysts
rationale: An infosec agent needs deep integration with the enterprise's specific security stack (SIEM, firewalls, EDR, IAM, ticketing) and tuning to the organization's network topology, asset inventory, and risk posture. While the skills and frameworks are standardized across companies, deploying an agent requires substantial per-enterprise configuration of detection rules, escalation paths, and compliance controls. Same product, many enterprise integrations.

title: Instructional coordinators
rationale: Instructional coordinators' work centers on relationships with teachers, principals, and administrators—mentoring, coaching, observing classrooms, and negotiating curriculum changes through trust built over time. While an agent could augment portable parts (analyzing test data, drafting curriculum materials, summarizing research on teaching techniques), the core relational work of persuading and developing teachers stays with the human. Per-district context also matters but the binding constraint is interpersonal.

title: Insurance sales agents
rationale: Insurance sales is fundamentally relational — agents build trust with clients, generate referrals, and maintain ongoing relationships for renewals and claims support. An AI agent could automate quoting, policy comparison, application processing, and follow-ups (T2-style with carrier system integrations), but the core client acquisition and trust-building stays with the human. The relational core dominates the value proposition, especially for independent agents who compete on advice and rapport.

title: Insurance underwriters
rationale: Underwriting agents require deep integration with each carrier's policy administration systems, rating engines, proprietary risk models, and underwriting guidelines that vary by line of business and state regulations. While the core skills (risk analysis, applying guidelines) are similar across insurers, deployment requires per-enterprise configuration of decision rules, escalation logic, and connections to internal data sources and external feeds (MIB, credit bureaus, MVR).

title: Interior designers
rationale: Interior design is heavily relational — designers spend significant time soliciting clients, understanding their personal tastes and lifestyles, and coordinating with contractors and architects on-site. While an agent could help with CAD work, material specification, and renderings (portable tasks), the client relationship, on-site visits, and trust-based collaboration with builders remain with the human. Aesthetic judgment also has a tacit component, but the dominant constraint is the client-relationship core of the business.

title: Interpreters and translators
rationale: Translation has standard inputs (source text) and outputs (target text), with universal CAT tools and well-established workflows across industries. A horizontal AI agent (LLM-based translation) can perform this work without per-enterprise integration — just give it the documents. Live interpreting has performative/physical elements but the core knowledge work is genericizable.

title: Judges and hearing officers
rationale: Judicial decision-making relies on courtroom demeanor assessment, weighing credibility, judicial temperament, and discretion that even judges struggle to fully articulate. Beyond tacit gestalt, the role carries constitutional/societal authority that society won't delegate to an agent — though if one tried, only the judge could harness-engineer a tool to assist their own reasoning. Research, drafting opinions, and reviewing briefs can be augmented, but the core adjudicative judgment is genuinely tacit.

title: Labor relations specialists
rationale: The core of labor relations work is negotiating contracts, resolving grievances, and building trust between management, unions, and workers — value lives in established relationships and credibility with specific counterparties at the bargaining table. An agent could help draft contracts, analyze labor law, and prepare materials, but the relational negotiation and dispute resolution core stays with the human. Secondary tacit elements (reading the room in tense negotiations) also resist full extraction.

title: Landscape architects
rationale: Landscape architects use standard professional tools (CADD, GIS) to produce site plans, models, and specifications — inputs and outputs are largely standardized across firms. A horizontal agent connected to these standard design tools and project files could perform the design work without per-enterprise integration. Site-specific data (surveys, environmental reports) is project-level context rather than enterprise-level customization.

title: Lawyers
rationale: Lawyers' work requires deep integration with firm-specific document management systems, matter databases, precedent libraries, and client files, plus tuning to jurisdiction and practice-area workflows—Harvey-style deployments require months of per-firm configuration. While the core skills (research, drafting, analysis) are similar across firms, the agent product needs custom integrations and workflow tuning per enterprise. Senior partner work with key client relationships would shade toward T3c, but the bulk of lawyer work (associates doing research, drafting, document review) fits T2.

title: Librarians and library media specialists
rationale: Librarian work involves significant physical/in-person components (story time, circulation desk, patron interaction) but the knowledge core—cataloging, reference research, collection management—requires integration with each library's specific ILS (Integrated Library System like Alma, Sierra, Koha), local catalog, patron database, and consortium resources. An agent could provide reference and cataloging assistance generically, but enterprise deployment requires per-institution integration with collection systems and tuning to local collection policies and patron populations.

title: Library technicians and assistants
rationale: The role is primarily physical and clerical: checking materials in/out, reshelving books, staffing service desks, and helping patrons in person. While it includes some information-organization tasks, the core work requires physical presence in the library.

title: Loan officers
rationale: Loan officer work requires deep integration with each lender's loan origination system (LOS), underwriting software, credit bureau feeds, compliance/regulatory rules, and product-specific terms — all of which vary by institution. An AI agent would be a horizontal product (analyze applications, verify documents, run underwriting logic, communicate with applicants) but every deployment requires connecting to the bank's core systems and tuning to its credit policies and state/federal compliance workflows. The relational/sales aspects (especially in mortgage and commercial) add some T3c flavor, but the bulk of the role — underwriting and processing — fits T2.

title: Logisticians
rationale: A logistician agent requires deep integration with enterprise-specific ERP, WMS, TMS, and procurement systems (SAP, Oracle, Manhattan), plus tuning to each company's supplier network, SKUs, routing rules, and escalation workflows. The skills are generic but every deployment demands months of integration and configuration to the company's supply chain data and processes. This matches T2: same agent product, custom per-enterprise configuration.

title: Management analysts
rationale: Management consulting blends tacit diagnostic judgment with deep client relationships — proposals are won, access to personnel is granted, and recommendations are accepted largely on the basis of trust with specific executives. An AI agent can do significant portions of the analytical work (data analysis, benchmarking, deck drafting) but the relational core of winning engagements, interviewing personnel, and persuading managers to adopt changes stays with the human. The portable analytic pieces resemble T1 work, but the role's value capture is fundamentally relational.

title: Marine engineers and naval architects
rationale: Marine engineering and naval architecture involves deep tacit judgment about hull behavior, structural integrity, and propulsion tradeoffs that even experts struggle to fully articulate, combined with safety-critical physical intuition refined over decades. While CAD and simulation tools are standard, the design judgment for one-off vessels and offshore structures requires close expert iteration rather than a horizontal or configurable agent. An agent for this work would need to be harness-engineered by the senior engineers themselves alongside specialized simulation tools.

title: Market research analysts
rationale: An AI agent for market research analysts needs access to enterprise-specific data sources (CRM, sales data, customer databases, survey platforms like Qualtrics, social listening tools) and tuning to the company's products, competitive landscape, and reporting conventions. While the analytical skills are generic, deployment requires per-enterprise integration with proprietary data and workflows, plus eval dashboards trusted by internal stakeholders. This matches the T2 pattern of a generic framework with substantial per-customer configuration.

title: Material recording clerks
rationale: The work involves tracking inventory and shipments in enterprise systems (WMS, ERP, RFID scanners) that vary by employer. An agent automating this requires per-enterprise integrations into the specific inventory/ERP stack and workflow tuning, similar to other back-office automation. Also includes significant physical warehouse handling, but the recording/scheduling core is T2 knowledge work.

title: Materials engineers
rationale: Materials engineering work depends heavily on proprietary processes, lab equipment, physical testing, and tacit expertise about how materials behave in specific manufacturing contexts. Much of the judgment (failure analysis, material selection trade-offs) is built from hands-on experience with specific products and is hard to fully articulate; an agent would need close iteration with the expert and integration with physical experimentation. Relational and physical lab elements further prevent a generic or merely-configured agent from doing the work.

title: Mathematicians and statisticians
rationale: An AI agent for this work uses standard statistical/mathematical tooling (R, Python, SAS) but needs deep integration with each enterprise's specific data sources, schemas, and domain context (clinical trial protocols, government survey designs, manufacturing QA pipelines) to deliver useful analyses. The analytical skills are portable, but the value comes from being wired into the organization's data infrastructure and tuned to its reporting conventions and regulatory requirements. This matches the T2 pattern of a genericizable agent framework requiring substantial per-enterprise configuration.

title: Mechanical engineers
rationale: Mechanical engineering work depends on physical intuition, manufacturability judgment, and integration with hardware/prototyping that involves genuinely tacit expertise — the senior engineer's sense for whether a design will fail under load or be manufacturable. While CAD tools are standardized, the design judgment, multi-physics tradeoffs, and physical testing loops require expert-driven harness engineering rather than a generic or configurable agent. Some sub-tasks (drafting, simulation setup) lean T1, but the core role is dominated by tacit physical-world judgment.

title: Medical dosimetrists
rationale: Medical dosimetry is knowledge work centered on radiation dose calculation and treatment planning using specialized software (Eclipse, Pinnacle, RayStation) integrated with hospital imaging systems (CT/MRI), EHRs, and linear accelerator hardware. An agent would need deep per-enterprise integration with each cancer center's planning systems, equipment calibration data, and physician workflows, plus regulatory/QA compliance — similar to how Abridge integrates per-hospital. The skills are standardized across institutions but deployment requires substantial customization, making this T2.

title: Medical records specialists
rationale: Medical records work requires deep integration with the enterprise's specific EHR system (Epic, Cerner, etc.), billing systems, and coding workflows, plus tuning to the facility's documentation patterns and payer rules. The skills are standardized (ICD-10, CPT coding) but an agent like Abridge or similar needs per-hospital integration, compliance/HIPAA configuration, and escalation logic to clinicians. Same agent product, but every deployment requires substantial integration work.

title: Medical scientists
rationale: Medical scientists do frontier research requiring deep tacit expertise — hypothesis generation, experimental design intuition, and interpretation of ambiguous results rely on judgment that even expert scientists cannot fully articulate. While portions (literature review, statistical analysis, grant drafting) are genericizable, the core scientific judgment requires the expert themselves to harness-engineer an agent through close iteration. Lab-specific protocols and research directions vary too idiosyncratically for a horizontal or framework-based agent product.

title: Medical transcriptionists
rationale: An AI agent for medical transcription (like Abridge) needs deep integration with the specific hospital's EHR system, custom templates for different physician specialties and document types, and tuning to local terminology and workflows. The core skill is generic but enterprise deployment requires substantial per-customer integration work into clinical systems with HIPAA compliance.

title: Models
rationale: Modeling is primarily performative/physical work — posing, displaying clothing, and embodying a physical appearance for photographers and audiences. It is not knowledge work.

title: Music directors and composers
rationale: Music direction and composition are creative/performative knowledge work where value comes from artistic taste, interpretive vision, and ensemble leadership — tacit qualities that even the artist can't fully articulate. An agent could assist with arrangement drafting or score prep, but the core artistic judgment must be iteratively shaped by the individual composer/director. There's no enterprise integration story; deployment would be per-artist harness work.

title: Natural sciences managers
rationale: Natural sciences managers lead research teams, hire and evaluate scientists, secure budgets, and communicate with executives, clients, and policymakers — value lives in relationships, leadership, and accountability for high-stakes scientific decisions. Portable work (drafting reports, reviewing literature, scheduling) can be augmented by horizontal agents, but the managerial core depends on trust, organizational standing, and judgment that cannot be transferred to an agent. Some tacit scientific judgment also factors in, but the dominant constraint is relational/leadership authority.

title: Network and computer systems administrators
rationale: A sysadmin AI agent requires deep integration into each enterprise's specific network topology, server inventory, identity providers, monitoring tools, ticketing systems, and security policies. The core skills (Linux/Windows admin, networking, troubleshooting) are generic, but deploying an agent to actually manage an organization's infrastructure requires substantial per-customer configuration, credentialed access, and tuning to local conventions and escalation paths.

title: News analysts, reporters, and journalists
rationale: Journalism's core value lies in cultivated source relationships, on-the-ground interviewing, and trust-based access to people willing to share tips and leads—these relational assets stay with the human reporter. An AI agent can substantially augment portable parts (research, drafting, editing, transcription, multimedia production) but cannot replace the human who has cultivated the contacts and conducts in-person interviews. The work is also partially tacit (editorial judgment, news instinct), but the binding constraint is relational.

title: Nuclear engineers
rationale: Nuclear engineering involves deep tacit expertise around reactor physics, safety judgment, and regulatory compliance specific to particular facilities and equipment, where mistakes are catastrophic and judgment is hard to fully articulate. An agent would need to be built by the experts themselves through close iteration on plant-specific systems, with senior engineers in the loop validating safety-critical decisions. Heavy regulatory and physical-plant context makes this resistant to either horizontal or framework-based deployment.

title: Operations research analysts
rationale: OR analysts solve enterprise-specific optimization problems requiring deep integration with company databases, ERP/supply chain systems, and proprietary business processes. The analytical methods (forecasting, simulation, optimization) are generic, but agent deployment needs per-enterprise data connectors, domain context, and tuned outputs trusted by managers — fitting the T2 pattern of generic framework with substantial custom configuration.

title: Paralegals and legal assistants
rationale: A paralegal AI agent (like Harvey) needs deep per-firm integration with document management systems (iManage, NetDocuments), case management tools, court e-filing systems, and firm-specific templates and workflows. While the underlying skills (legal research, drafting) are similar across firms, each deployment requires substantial configuration to firm precedents, practice area conventions, and matter files. Same product, custom configuration per enterprise.

title: Personal financial advisors
rationale: Personal financial advisors' value is fundamentally relational — clients choose advisors based on trust built over years, and the work involves making clients 'feel comfortable' with their financial decisions and life circumstances. An AI agent can augment the portable analytical work (portfolio analysis, research, plan drafting, monitoring) but the relational core of client meetings, trust-building, and sales/networking stays with the human advisor.

title: Photographers
rationale: Photography is primarily a physical/performative craft requiring being on location with a camera to capture images of people, places, and events. While post-production editing is knowledge work, the core job is physical image capture that cannot be done by a software agent.

title: Physicists and astronomers
rationale: Frontier physics and astronomy research depends on deeply tacit expert intuition about which theories to pursue, how to design experiments, and how to interpret anomalous results — knowledge that even leading researchers cannot fully articulate. An agent for this work must be harness-engineered by the scientist themselves, iterating closely on their specific research program rather than deployed as a generic or enterprise-configured product.

title: Political scientists
rationale: Political scientists do research work—gathering public data, analyzing policies, writing reports—using standard tools (documents, public datasets, survey data). A horizontal research/analysis agent with access to the researcher's files and public sources can do this work without per-enterprise integration. The outputs (reports, analyses, forecasts) are standard across employers.

title: Postsecondary education administrators
rationale: Postsecondary administrators (deans, provosts, admissions officers, student affairs) derive most of their value from relationships with faculty, students, parents, and external stakeholders, plus institution-specific governance and politics. An agent could automate portable pieces (application screening, registrar data processing, scheduling) but the core advising, judgment calls on admissions/tenure, and stakeholder management stay with humans. The relational and institutional-political core dominates, making this T3c.

title: Postsecondary teachers
rationale: Postsecondary teaching combines relational mentorship (advising students, dissertation supervision, committee work, building scholarly networks) with genuinely tacit research expertise in a specialized subfield. While portable tasks like syllabus drafting, lecture prep, and grading could be handled by a horizontal agent, the core value—mentoring specific students, peer-reviewed original research, and institutional governance—depends on established relationships and credentialed standing that stay with the human.

title: Producers and directors
rationale: Producers and directors combine relational work (assembling cast/crew, raising money, managing talent relationships) with deeply tacit creative judgment (interpreting scripts, shaping performance, defining a production's look and feel). The relational and reputational core — who you know, who trusts your vision, who will finance and act for you — cannot be transferred to an agent, though agents can help with scheduling, budgeting, research, and drafting. T3c fits best given the centrality of relationships with talent, financiers, and crew, with tacit creative gestalt as a secondary T3b element.

title: Project management specialists
rationale: Project management is fundamentally relational and coordinative — value comes from established trust with clients, stakeholder management, navigating internal politics, and resolving ambiguity through human conversations. An agent could automate the portable artifacts (Gantt charts, status reports, budget tracking, risk registers) but the core work of aligning people, negotiating scope changes, and being the trusted point of contact stays with the human. Tools and integrations vary heavily (Jira, MS Project, Primavera, SAP) but the binding constraint isn't tool integration — it's the relational/judgment core.

title: Property appraisers and assessors
rationale: Property appraisal involves substantial site inspection and physical observation, but the analytical/valuation core (comparables analysis, report writing, mass appraisal modeling) is knowledge work. An AI agent for this role needs integration with jurisdiction-specific assessment databases, MLS/comparable sales data, GIS systems, and locality-specific tax assessment rules, plus configuration to defend valuations under each jurisdiction's appeals process. The skills are similar across appraisers, but each enterprise/locality deployment requires meaningful integration and rule tuning, making it T2.

title: Public relations and fundraising managers
rationale: PR and fundraising management centers on relationships—with journalists, donors, executives, and community stakeholders—and on representing an organization's reputation in high-stakes, trust-dependent contexts. An AI agent can draft press releases, prepare grant applications, research donors, and analyze media trends, but the core value of in-person donor cultivation, media rapport, and crisis judgment stays with the human manager.

title: Public relations specialists
rationale: PR work depends heavily on relationships with specific journalists, media contacts, and executive stakeholders, plus deep knowledge of the client's voice, ongoing narrative, and sensitive internal context. An agent can draft press releases, monitor social sentiment, and prepare briefing materials, but the relational core—pitching reporters, managing crisis communications, and speaking on behalf of executives—remains with the human. While some tacit brand/voice knowledge could be documented (T3a-like), the persistent value lies in trusted relationships.

title: Public safety telecommunicators
rationale: A 911 dispatcher AI agent requires deep per-PSAP integration: computer-aided dispatch (CAD) systems, local geography/GIS, agency-specific protocols, radio channel management, and connections to local police/fire/EMS units. The skill of triaging emergency calls is similar across jurisdictions, but each deployment needs custom integrations and protocol tuning, plus heavy compliance/escalation logic — classic T2 shape.

title: Purchasing managers, buyers, and purchasing agents
rationale: A procurement agent needs deep integration into the enterprise's ERP/procurement systems (SAP Ariba, Coupa, Oracle), supplier databases, contract repositories, and inventory systems, plus configuration around the company's approval workflows, vendor relationships, and category-specific policies. The skills are similar across companies, but each deployment requires substantial per-enterprise integration and tuning. Supplier relationships add some T3c flavor for senior roles, but the bulk of buyer/agent work is system-mediated transactional procurement.

title: Receptionists
rationale: A receptionist agent would need to integrate with each enterprise's specific phone system, calendar/scheduling software, visitor management, EHR or CRM, and be tuned to the organization's escalation rules and information disclosure policies. The skills are generic but the deployment requires per-customer integrations and configuration (similar to customer service agents). Physical greeting is partially being replaced by kiosks/digital check-in, which fits the T2 deployment pattern.

title: Sales engineers
rationale: Sales engineers combine technical product expertise with relationship-driven selling — their value lies in established customer trust, tailoring solutions to specific buyer contexts, and negotiating deals. An AI agent can assist with technical demos, RFP responses, and product configuration, but the customer relationship, in-person presentations, and negotiation must remain with the human. The relational core makes this T3c.

title: Sales managers
rationale: Sales managers' work centers on leading, motivating, and developing sales teams, plus maintaining relationships with key customers, distributors, and cross-functional stakeholders. While analytics, forecasting, and pipeline review can be automated with CRM-integrated agents (T2-like), the core leadership and relational components — coaching reps, closing escalated deals, internal political navigation — stay with the human.

title: Secretaries and administrative assistants
rationale: An AI agent for this role needs deep integration with the enterprise's calendar, email, CRM, document management, and (for medical/legal subspecialties) EHR or case management systems, plus tuning to the specific executive's or office's preferences and workflows. The skills are generic across companies but deployment requires substantial per-enterprise configuration of integrations and escalation logic, mirroring the customer service agent pattern.

title: Securities, commodities, and financial services sales agents
rationale: The core value of brokers, investment bankers, and financial sales agents lies in client relationships, trust, and book-of-business — they cold-call, network, and maintain long-term advisory ties with individuals and corporate executives. An AI agent can augment portable parts (market analysis, deal modeling, pitch deck drafting, compliance checks) but cannot replace the relational core required to win and retain clients in high-stakes financial transactions. This places the role firmly in T3c.

title: Set and exhibit designers
rationale: Set and exhibit designers use standard professional tools (CAD programs, sketching software) to produce designs based on scripts, briefs, and project parameters. An AI agent could perform much of the design work using these same standard tools without requiring enterprise-specific integration—each project is essentially self-contained with portable inputs and outputs. The work is largely freelance/per-project, reinforcing that no deep enterprise customization is needed.

title: Sociologists
rationale: Sociological research uses standard methods (surveys, interviews, statistical analysis) and standard tools (SPSS, R, Stata, qualitative coding software) that are consistent across employers. An AI agent could conduct literature reviews, design studies, analyze data, and draft reports using these generic tools without per-enterprise integration. The work product (papers, reports, policy analyses) is portable across institutions.

title: Software developers, quality assurance analysts, and testers
rationale: Software development uses standard tools (git, IDEs, file system) that horizontal coding agents like Claude Code can access at any company without per-enterprise integration. The codebase itself provides the context; no months-long configuration is needed to deploy the agent at a new company.

title: Special effects artists and animators
rationale: Special effects artists and animators use standard professional tools (Maya, Blender, After Effects, Houdini, Unreal) that are common across studios, with project files accessed through standard file systems. An AI agent could be built once to operate these tools and produce assets at any studio without per-enterprise integration. While studios have proprietary pipelines, the customization is minimal — analogous to a software developer's codebase access.

title: Survey researchers
rationale: Survey research uses standard tools (statistical software like SPSS/R/Stata, survey platforms like Qualtrics) and produces standard outputs (survey designs, statistical analyses, reports) that are similar across employers. A horizontal AI agent with access to these tools and the project files could design surveys, run statistical analyses, and write up results without substantial per-enterprise integration. The work resembles a generic data analyst more than a deeply embedded enterprise role.

title: Tax examiners and collectors, and revenue agents
rationale: An AI agent for this role would need deep integration with government tax systems (IRS IMF/BMF, state revenue databases, case management systems), access to taxpayer records, and configuration tuned to specific agency enforcement procedures and legal compliance requirements. The skills (reviewing returns, applying tax code, collections workflows) are similar across agencies, but deploying an agent requires substantial per-agency integration into proprietary tax infrastructure with strict compliance/audit controls. This matches the T2 pattern of a common product framework with heavy enterprise-specific configuration.

title: Technical writers
rationale: Technical writing requires deep familiarity with a specific company's products, internal terminology, style guides, and source materials (engineer interviews, product specs, code). An agent needs access to this proprietary knowledge and documentation systems, but it's documentable through artifact analysis and structured extraction from SMEs, after which the work becomes a T2-style deployment with integrations into the company's docs platforms and product repositories.

title: Tellers
rationale: Teller work is primarily physical cash handling and in-person customer service at a branch counter, not knowledge work. The role centers on processing physical transactions, counting cash, and face-to-face interactions, which fall outside the scope of agent-deployable knowledge work.

title: Training and development managers
rationale: Training and development managers' core work involves aligning training with organizational goals, conferring with department heads and executives to identify needs, managing staff, and overseeing budgets — work that depends on internal relationships, political context, and stakeholder buy-in specific to the organization. An agent could draft training materials, evaluate vendors, and analyze effectiveness, but the relational and leadership core (negotiating priorities with executives, motivating staff, building cross-functional consensus) stays with the human. Tacit organizational knowledge could partly be extracted, but the persistent value lives in ongoing relationships with leaders and subject-matter experts.

title: Training and development specialists
rationale: Training specialists must assess organization-specific needs, design materials tailored to internal processes/policies, and deliver instruction tied to a company's culture and systems. Much of this tacit context (workflows, compliance requirements, role-specific skills) can be extracted via interviews and artifact analysis, then converted into a T2-style configured agent. Pure horizontal authoring tools aren't enough because the content is deeply enterprise-specific.

title: Travel agents
rationale: Travel agent work uses standard industry reservation systems (GDS like Sabre/Amadeus), supplier portals, and CRM systems that require per-agency integration and configuration. An AI agent would need connectors to these booking systems, agency-specific preferred-supplier rules, commission structures, and client profile databases, plus tuned escalation logic for travel disruptions. The skills are generic but enterprise deployment requires substantial integration work.

title: Tutors
rationale: Tutoring uses standard educational content (curricula, practice problems, standardized test materials) that is consistent across students and institutions. A horizontal AI agent like ChatGPT/Khanmigo can deliver tutoring directly to students without per-enterprise integration — the 'customization' is just adapting to the individual student's level. No deep institutional system integration is required.

title: Urban and regional planners
rationale: Urban planning's core value involves stakeholder relationships—with public officials, developers, community groups, and residents—through meetings, hearings, and negotiated consensus-building over local political dynamics. An AI agent could handle portable tasks like GIS analysis, zoning research, data gathering, and drafting reports, but the relational and political work of building stakeholder trust and navigating community politics stays with the human planner. While there's also tacit local knowledge (T3a-like), the relational/political core of the role is the binding constraint.

title: Web developers and digital designers
rationale: Web development and digital design use standard tools (VS Code, Figma, browsers, git) across companies, and a horizontal AI agent like Claude Code or a design copilot can do the work by accessing the project's codebase and design files without per-enterprise integration. The work is genericizable — skills and outputs (HTML/CSS/JS, mockups) are standardized industry-wide.

title: Wholesale and manufacturing sales representatives
rationale: B2B sales value lives in established buyer relationships, trust, and long-cycle account management with specific customers. An agent can handle lead research, prospecting outreach, quote prep, CRM updates, and follow-ups, but the relational core—negotiating, building rapport, closing—stays with the human rep.

title: Writers and authors
rationale: Writing is a generic knowledge task with standard inputs (briefs, research) and outputs (prose) using standard tools (word processors, CMSs). A horizontal LLM-based agent can perform this work at any organization without per-enterprise integration — just provide the brief and style guide.
